content:
# James Chapter 2 — The LOGOS Bible (Formal)
**Translator:** LOGOS | **Source:** Codex Sinaiticus (Aleph, 01), page 263 | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/JAS_002_page_001.jpg

1. My brothers, do not hold the faith of our Lord Jesus Christ, the Lord of glory, with partiality toward persons.

2. For if a man wearing a gold ring and fine clothing comes into your assembly, and a poor man in filthy clothing also comes in,

3. and you look upon the one wearing the fine clothing and say, "You sit here in a good place," and to the poor man you say, "You stand there," or "Sit below my footstool,"

4. have you not made distinctions among yourselves and become judges with evil reasonings?

5. Listen, my beloved brothers: did not God choose the poor of the world to be rich in faith and heirs of the kingdom which he promised to those who love him?

6. But you have dishonored the poor man. Do not the rich oppress you, and do they not themselves drag you into courts?

7. Do they not blaspheme the good name that was invoked over you?

8. If indeed you fulfill the royal law according to the Scripture, "You shall love your neighbor as yourself," you do well.

9. But if you show partiality toward persons, you commit sin, being convicted by the law as transgressors.

10. For whoever keeps the whole law but stumbles in one point has become guilty of all.

11. For he who said, "Do not commit adultery," also said, "Do not murder." Now if you do not commit adultery but you do murder, you have become a transgressor of the law.

12. So speak and so act as those who are to be judged by the law of liberty.

13. For judgment is without mercy to the one who has shown no mercy. Mercy triumphs over judgment.

14. What is the profit, my brothers, if someone says he has faith but does not have works? Is the faith able to save him?

15. If a brother or sister is naked and lacking daily food,

16. and one of you says to them, "Go in peace, be warmed and be filled," but you do not give them the things needful for the body, what is the profit?

17. So also faith, if it does not have works, is dead by itself.

18. But someone will say, "You have faith, and I have works." Show me your faith apart from the works, and I will show you faith from my works.

19. You believe that God is one. You do well; the demons also believe, and shudder.

20. But do you wish to know, O empty man, that faith apart from works is idle?

21. Was not Abraham our father justified by works when he offered up Isaac his son upon the altar?

22. You see that faith was working together with his works, and by the works the faith was made complete.

23. And the Scripture was fulfilled that says, "Abraham believed God, and it was reckoned to him as righteousness," and he was called a friend of God.

24. You see that a man is justified by works, and not by faith alone.

25. And likewise was not also Rahab the harlot justified by works, when she received the messengers and sent them out by another way?

26. For just as the body apart from the spirit is dead, so also faith apart from works is dead.

---
## LOGOS Translation Notes

1. **v.1 — "Partiality toward persons" (prosopolempsiais):** Sinaiticus reads prosopolempsiais, a compound noun from prosopon (face) + lambanein (to receive). Rendered with formal precision as "partiality toward persons" to preserve the etymology of face-regard. The genitive tou kyriou hemon Iesou Christou tēs doxēs is retained in apposition: "the Lord of glory" modifies "our Lord Jesus Christ."

2. **v.2 — "Assembly" (synagoge):** Sinaiticus uses synagōgēn, not ekklēsia. James alone in the NT uses "synagogue" for the Christian gathering, reflecting the Jewish-Christian character of his audience. Translated literally as "assembly" to preserve the lexical distinction from "church."

3. **v.4 — "Judges with evil reasonings" (kritai dialogismōn ponērōn):** The genitive is qualitative — judges characterized by wicked deliberations. The formal rendering preserves the Greek construction rather than smoothing to "unjust judges."

4. **v.5 — "The poor of the world" (tous ptōchous tō kosmō):** Sinaiticus reads the dative tō kosmō, indicating "poor with respect to the world" rather than the genitive tou kosmou ("of the world") found in some witnesses. The dative is rendered "of the world" for readability while noting the Sinaiticus form is a dative of reference.

5. **v.13 — "Mercy triumphs over judgment" (katakauchatai eleos kriseōs):** The verb katakauchatai (boasts against, exults over) is striking — mercy does not merely temper judgment but prevails victoriously over it. This is the theological climax of the partiality discourse.

6. **v.14 — "Is the faith able to save him?" (mē dunatai hē pistis sōsai auton):** The particle mē expects a negative answer: "The faith cannot save him, can it?" The article hē before pistis is significant — it is "the faith" (that particular faith without works), not faith generically. Sinaiticus clearly reads hē pistis with the article.

7. **v.18 — Textual variant:** Sinaiticus reads "Show me your faith apart from the works" (chōris tōn ergōn), where some later manuscripts read "from your works" (ek tōn ergōn sou). The Sinaiticus reading sharpens the challenge: faith without any works at all cannot be demonstrated.

8. **v.19 — "God is one" (heis estin ho theos):** James affirms the Shema (Deut. 6:4). The demons' orthodox theology coupled with their shuddering (phrissousin) demonstrates that mere intellectual assent without obedient action is insufficient — even demonic.

9. **v.20 — "Idle" (argē):** Sinaiticus reads argē (idle, useless) rather than the majority text nekra (dead). This is a distinctive Sinaiticus reading. The word argē (a-ergē, "without work") creates a wordplay with erga (works): faith without works is literally "workless." This paronomasia is preserved in the formal translation.

10. **v.23 — "Friend of God" (philos theou):** Abraham alone in Scripture receives this title (cf. 2 Chr. 20:7; Isa. 41:8). The passive "was called" (eklēthē) indicates divine designation, not merely human recognition.

11. **v.25 — "Rahab the harlot" (Raab hē pornē):** Sinaiticus spells the name Raab. The formal translation retains "harlot" (pornē) rather than softening to "prostitute" or "innkeeper," as the moral contrast heightens the theological point: justification by works reaches even to the most unlikely.

12. **v.26 — Body-spirit analogy:** The closing verse provides the chapter's summary metaphor. As the body without the animating spirit (pneumatos) is a corpse, so faith without the animating principle of works is dead (nekra). This forms an inclusio with v.17, bracketing the entire argument.

— LOGOS, AI Translator
